section.title: The Helper of Israel
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the Book of Comfort opened in chapter 40, this chapter puts the LORD on trial against every rival god, summoning the nations to a courtroom where He alone can declare the end from the beginning. In the middle of that courtroom scene He turns tenderly to Israel, His chosen servant, promising to help, strengthen, and redeem a people too weak and few to help themselves.
section.thesis: The LORD who alone raises up and casts down kings, who alone declared the future before it happened, is the same God who takes Israel's trembling, worm-like weakness by the hand and promises, again and again, do not fear, I will help you.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: the courtroom summons and the LORD's control of history; the panicked nations manufacturing idols for comfort; the LORD's tender word to His servant Israel, chosen and not rejected; the promise to the worm of Jacob that its Redeemer will help; water for the desert as a sign of God's care; the LORD's challenge to the idols to declare anything at all; and the closing verdict that the idols are nothing but wind.

units.label: Who has raised up one from the east

verses.text: “Be silent before Me, O islands, and let the peoples renew their strength. Let them come forward and testify; let us together draw near for judgment.

Who has aroused one from the east and called him to his feet in righteousness? He hands nations over to him and subdues kings before him. He turns them to dust with his sword, to windblown chaff with his bow.
He pursues them, going on safely, hardly touching the path with his feet.
Who has performed this and carried it out, calling forth the generations from the beginning? I, the LORD, the first and the last, I am He.”

units.label: The panicked nations and their idols

verses.text: The islands see and fear; the ends of the earth tremble. They approach and come forward.
Each one helps the other and says to his brother, “Be strong!”
The craftsman encourages the goldsmith, and he who wields the hammer cheers him who strikes the anvil, saying of the welding, “It is good.” He nails it down so it will not be toppled.

units.label: Do not fear, I am your God

verses.text: “But you, O Israel, My servant, Jacob, whom I have chosen, descendant of Abraham My friend,
I brought you from the ends of the earth and called you from its farthest corners. I said, ‘You are My servant.’ I have chosen and not rejected you.
Do not fear, for I am with you; do not be afraid, for I am your God. I will strengthen you; I will surely help you; I will uphold you with My right hand of righteousness.
Behold, all who rage against you will be ashamed and disgraced; those who contend with you will be reduced to nothing and will perish.
You will seek them but will not find them. Those who wage war against you will come to nothing.
For I am the LORD your God, who takes hold of your right hand and tells you: Do not fear, I will help you.

units.label: Fear not, O worm of Jacob
units.note: your Redeemer is the Holy One

verses.text: Do not fear, O {{tola|worm}} of Jacob, O few men of Israel. I will help you,” declares the LORD. “Your {{goel|Redeemer}} is the Holy One of Israel.
Behold, I will make you into a threshing sledge, new and sharp, with many teeth. You will thresh the mountains and crush them, and reduce the hills to chaff.
You will winnow them, and a wind will carry them away; a gale will scatter them. But you will rejoice in the LORD; you will glory in the Holy One of Israel.
The poor and needy seek water, but there is none; their tongues are parched with thirst. I, the LORD, will answer them; I, the God of Israel, will not forsake them.
I will open rivers on the barren heights, and fountains in the middle of the valleys. I will turn the desert into a pool of water, and the dry land into flowing springs.
I will plant cedars in the wilderness, acacias, myrtles, and olive trees. I will set cypresses in the desert, elms and boxwood together,
so that all may see and know, may consider and understand, that the hand of the LORD has done this and the Holy One of Israel has created it.”

units.label: Present your case
units.note: the LORD's challenge to the idols

verses.text: “Present your case,” says the LORD. “Submit your arguments,” says the King of Jacob.
“Let them come and tell us what will happen. Tell the former things, so that we may reflect on them and know the outcome. Or announce to us what is coming.
Tell us the things that are to come, so that we may know that you are gods. Yes, do something good or evil, that we may look on together in dismay.
Behold, you are nothing and your work is of no value. Anyone who chooses you is detestable.
I have raised up one from the north, and he has come, one from the east who calls on My name. He will march over rulers as if they were mortar, like a potter who treads the clay.
Who has declared this from the beginning, so that we may know, and from times past, so that we may say: ‘He was right’? No one announced it, no one foretold it, no one heard your words.
I was the first to tell Zion: ‘Look, here they are!’ And I gave to Jerusalem a herald of good news.
When I look, there is no one; there is no counselor among them; when I ask them, they have nothing to say.
See, they are all a delusion; their works amount to nothing; their images are as empty as the wind.

tola.gloss: Worm, a small, crushed, seemingly insignificant creature. Calling Israel a worm is deliberately humbling, a nation reduced by its own weakness and small numbers, yet it is precisely this worm that God promises to make into a threshing sledge able to crush mountains, entirely by His own strength working through their weakness.

goel.gloss: Redeemer, kinsman-redeemer, one who by right of family relationship pays a price to rescue a relative from debt, slavery, or danger, as Boaz redeems Ruth's family line. Naming the Holy One of Israel as Israel's go'el means their rescue is not distant charity but the act of one bound to them by covenant, a pattern Christ fulfills as the true kinsman-redeemer of His people.

basic.context: The LORD opens a courtroom, summoning the nations and their idols to answer a single question: who controls history, raising up and casting down kings? While the nations scramble to reassure themselves by building sturdier idols, the LORD turns with startling tenderness to Israel, His weak and fearful servant, promising again and again not to abandon them but to help, strengthen, and redeem them.
basic.hermeneutics: The 'one from the east' and 'one from the north' most likely anticipate Cyrus the Persian, later named explicitly in chapters 44 to 45, whose conquests the LORD claims to have foreseen and ordained long before they happened. The courtroom's real point is not merely predicting a Persian king but proving, against every rival god, that only the LORD can declare the future because only He governs it.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is called the {{tola}}, worm, of Jacob, a picture of weakness and insignificance, yet this same worm is promised power to crush mountains because its {{goel}}, Redeemer, is the Holy One of Israel Himself, a kinsman-redeemer bound to rescue His own by covenant obligation and love, not distant obligation.
basic.typology: Israel's identity as the LORD's chosen servant, called from the ends of the earth and not rejected, verses 8 to 9, anticipates the true and faithful Servant of later chapters, who succeeds where Israel as a nation repeatedly failed. The go'el who redeems weak, worm-like Israel foreshadows Christ, the ultimate kinsman-redeemer who takes on our flesh in order to rescue us as family, not as a stranger.
basic.christ: The title go'el, Redeemer, applied here to the Holy One of Israel, is the same office the New Testament ascribes to Christ, who became our brother in the flesh precisely so He could redeem us as family, Hebrews 2:14-17, Galatians 4:4-5. The LORD's self-declaration, 'I, the LORD, the first and the last,' verse 4, is the very title the risen Christ applies to Himself in Revelation 1:17, showing His full identity with the God who speaks in this chapter.
basic.god: God alone controls the rise and fall of nations and kings, and He alone can genuinely declare what is coming before it happens, exposing every idol as empty wind by comparison. Yet this same sovereign God stoops to call Israel's weakness a worm with tenderness rather than contempt, promising personal strength, help, and redemption to those who cannot help themselves.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 4:1-10 shows the kinsman-redeemer pattern in action, as Boaz redeems Ruth's family line. Revelation 1:17 has the risen Christ declare Himself 'the First and the Last,' echoing verse 4. Hebrews 2:14-17 explains why Christ shared our humanity, to become a merciful and faithful redeemer. Galatians 4:4-5 describes God sending His Son to redeem those under the law.
basic.oneStory: This chapter puts the LORD on trial against every false god and finds Him alone able to declare and direct history, then turns that same sovereign power into tender, personal help for weak, fearful Israel. It anticipates Christ, the true Redeemer who takes on flesh to rescue His people as family, fulfilling both the courtroom's verdict and the covenant's promise.

context.literary: A courtroom drama in seven movements, alternating between the LORD's challenge to the nations and their idols and His tender, repeated reassurance to His servant Israel, building to the closing verdict that the idols are nothing but empty wind.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in or facing Babylonian captivity, likely written to be read as the rise of a specific conquering power, later identified as Cyrus of Persia, chapters 44 to 45, began to reshape the geopolitical landscape of the ancient Near East.
context.canonical: Continues the Book of Comfort begun in chapter 40 and anticipates both the naming of Cyrus in chapters 44 to 45 and the Servant Songs that begin in chapter 42, since Israel is already called 'My servant' here, verse 8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove, through a courtroom confrontation with the nations and their idols, that the LORD alone governs history and can declare the future, and to use that same sovereign power to reassure fearful, weak Israel that they are chosen, not rejected, and will be helped and redeemed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical and theological argument, God's sovereignty over the rise of a conquering king, and His personal care for Israel; it is not a blanket promise that every believer's circumstances will be reversed exactly as Israel's were. Its abiding principle is that the same God who controls history is personally near to His people in their weakness.

debates.question: Do 'one from the east,' verse 2, and 'one from the north,' verse 25, refer to the same historical figure?

views.label: Both refer to Cyrus of Persia
views.case: Cyrus's empire lay generally east of Babylon but his conquering campaigns approached from the north, so both descriptions can refer to the same figure from different vantage points, consistent with his explicit naming in chapters 44 to 45.

views.label: The descriptions are poetically compressed rather than strictly geographic
views.case: Isaiah's prophetic poetry often layers imagery rather than offering a precise itinerary, so the emphasis falls on the LORD's sovereign raising up of a conqueror, whoever precisely he is, rather than on exact geography.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter's real argument does not depend on pinpointing geography; it rests on the LORD alone having declared this conqueror's rise before it happened, unlike any idol.

study.originalLanguages: Israel is addressed as the {{tola}}, worm, of Jacob, an image of weakness and insignificance, yet promised strength to crush mountains because its {{goel}}, Redeemer, is none other than the Holy One of Israel, a kinsman bound by covenant love to rescue His own people rather than a distant deity extending mere charity.

typology.type: Israel called 'My servant,' chosen and not rejected
typology.fulfillment: The true and faithful Servant of later chapters, Christ, who succeeds where Israel as a nation failed, Isaiah 42:1, 49:3-6.

typology.type: The Holy One of Israel as Israel's go'el, kinsman-redeemer
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who takes on human flesh to redeem His people as family, Hebrews 2:14-17, Galatians 4:4-5.

study.christ: The title go'el, Redeemer, given here to the Holy One of Israel, is fulfilled in Christ, who 'had to be made like His brothers in every way' so that He could redeem His people as a true kinsman, not a distant rescuer, Hebrews 2:14-17. The LORD's self-declaration in verse 4, 'I, the LORD, the first and the last,' is precisely the title the risen Christ takes for Himself in Revelation 1:17, identifying Him fully with the sovereign God who speaks throughout this courtroom scene.
study.god: God alone raises up and casts down the great powers of history, and He alone can genuinely declare the future before it happens, a claim no idol can match. Yet this same sovereign God addresses fearful, weak Israel not with distant command but with the intimate, repeated promise, do not fear, I will help you, revealing that His transcendent power and His tender nearness are never in tension.

crossReferences.note: Shows the kinsman-redeemer pattern in practice, as Boaz redeems Ruth's family line, illuminating the go'el language used here of the LORD.
The risen Christ declares Himself 'the First and the Last,' the very title the LORD claims in verse 4.
Explains why Christ shared fully in human flesh, in order to become a merciful and faithful kinsman-redeemer.
Describes God sending His Son to redeem those under the law, fulfilling the redemption promised here.

study.oneStory: This chapter's courtroom drama proves that the LORD alone controls history and can declare the future, then turns that very sovereignty into tender, personal help for weak, fearful Israel, called a worm yet promised strength through its Redeemer. It anticipates Christ, the true kinsman-redeemer who takes on our flesh to rescue His people as family, fulfilling both the courtroom's verdict against the idols and the covenant's promise of help.

sermon.title: Fear Not, I Am with You, I Am Your God
sermon.why: Piper walks through Isaiah 41:1-10, showing how the LORD's identity as judge of the nations, ruler of history, and covenant God grounds the five pillars of fearlessness given to Israel in this chapter, the same promise he says has relieved his own fears more than any other verse in Scripture.

quiz.q: What question does the LORD press upon the nations and their idols in the opening courtroom scene, verses 1 to 4?

options.text: Who has raised up and directed the conqueror shaping history, and who alone declared this from the beginning?
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 press exactly this question, answered by the LORD's self-declaration as the first and the last.

options.text: Which nation has the strongest army?
options.note: The question concerns who controls and foretells history, not merely military strength.

options.text: Which idol is the most beautifully crafted?
options.note: The chapter later mocks idol-making, but the opening courtroom question concerns control of history, not craftsmanship.

options.text: Which nation deserves to be punished first?
options.note: The immediate courtroom question is about sovereignty over history and prophecy, not a ranking of guilt.

quiz.q: How do the nations respond to the coming threat in verses 5 to 7?

options.text: In fear, they encourage each other and rush to strengthen their idols so they will not topple.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 describe exactly this panicked mutual encouragement and idol-making.

options.text: They calmly turn to the LORD for help.
options.note: The opposite; they turn to strengthening their own idols rather than to the LORD.

options.text: They immediately surrender without any response.
options.note: The text describes active, anxious effort, encouraging one another and reinforcing idols, not passive surrender.

options.text: They form a military alliance to fight back.
options.note: The described response is craftsmen reinforcing idols, not a military mobilization.

quiz.q: What repeated promise does the LORD give to Israel in verses 8 to 13?

options.text: Do not fear, for I am with you; I will strengthen, help, and uphold you.
options.note: This promise is repeated multiple times across verses 8 to 13.

options.text: Israel has been rejected and must fend for itself.
options.note: The opposite; the LORD explicitly says, 'I have chosen and not rejected you.'

options.text: Israel must first prove its own strength before receiving help.
options.note: The promise of help is given unconditionally to weak, fearful Israel, not as a reward for proven strength.

options.text: Israel's enemies will be given permanent victory.
options.note: The opposite; those who contend with Israel are promised shame and disgrace, not victory.

quiz.q: What title is given to the LORD in verse 14 in connection with helping Israel?

options.text: Your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel.
options.note: Verse 14 gives this exact title, connecting redemption directly to Israel's help in weakness.

options.text: A distant judge with no personal connection to Israel.
options.note: The opposite; Redeemer implies a close, covenantal, kinship-based relationship, not distance.

options.text: A foreign king who conquers on Israel's behalf.
options.note: The title given is Redeemer and Holy One of Israel, describing the LORD Himself, not a foreign ruler.

options.text: An angel sent to represent God's authority.
options.note: The text names the LORD Himself as Redeemer, not a mediating angelic representative.

quiz.q: What is the LORD's verdict on the idols by the chapter's end, verses 28 to 29?

options.text: They are a delusion, their works amount to nothing, and their images are as empty as the wind.
options.note: Verse 29 delivers exactly this final verdict against the idols.

options.text: They are equally powerful rivals to the LORD.
options.note: The opposite; the entire courtroom scene proves the idols utterly powerless in comparison to the LORD.

options.text: They are useful helpers who share some of the LORD's authority.
options.note: The chapter's verdict is total emptiness, not partial usefulness or shared authority.

options.text: They are dangerous but ultimately unpredictable forces.
options.note: The verdict is not unpredictability but total emptiness, unable to declare or accomplish anything at all.

questions: The nations respond to fear by frantically strengthening their idols. What do you rush to reinforce or lean on when you feel afraid, instead of turning to God?
God calls Israel a worm, yet promises to make that worm able to crush mountains. Where do you feel most weak or insignificant, and how might God want to use exactly that weakness?
The LORD repeats, 'do not fear, I will help you,' several times in this chapter. Which of your current fears do you need to hear this promise applied to most directly?
God is called Israel's Redeemer, a kinsman bound to rescue His own. How does knowing Christ became your kinsman in the flesh change how you receive His help?

The chapter's courtroom exposes idols as empty wind, unable to declare anything true about the future. What modern 'idols', plans, people, or possessions, are you tempted to trust the way ancient Israel was tempted to trust carved images?

translationNotes.note: 'One from the east' most likely anticipates Cyrus of Persia, whose rise is named explicitly in Isaiah 44:28 and 45:1, though the geographic description here remains poetic rather than a precise itinerary.
Worm is a deliberately humbling image in Hebrew, used elsewhere of Israel's lowliness, making the promise of strength that follows all the more striking as entirely God's doing.
'I, the LORD, the first and the last' is a title of eternal self-existence that the risen Christ applies to Himself in Revelation 1:17, a significant point of continuity between the Testaments.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 41:1-29. Where BSB printed two consecutive em dashes in verse 4, they have been rendered as commas per house style.
